splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps agency deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous provisions in the Sentencing Guidelines or criminal statutes.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether lenity should prevail over deference to agency interpretations in criminal contexts. At least three circuits hold that lenity trumps deference, while the Third Circuit has held that agency deference trumps lenity. The Supreme Court's decision in Loper Bright casts further doubt on the deference-first approach.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity prevails over agency deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal provisions, including Sentencing Guidelines, because lenity is a centuries-old canon that prevents agencies from expanding criminal liability.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency deference (Auer/Kisor) takes precedence over the rule of lenity when interpreting ambiguous Sentencing Guidelines commentary, allowing anti-defendant readings to stand when the agency interpretation is reasonable.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Sentencing Guidelines commentary (Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2) that expands the definition of 'controlled substance offense' to include inchoate offenses like conspiracy is entitled to controlling deference under Stinson/Kisor.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1, which adds conspiracy and attempt to the career-offender guideline's definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid authoritative interpretation entitled to deference. Some circuits defer to the commentary while others hold the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, making the commentary an impermissible expansion.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 reasonably interprets a genuinely ambiguous guideline and is entitled to controlling weight because it implicates the Sentencing Commission's substantive expertise.

positions.label: Commentary exceeds guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses like conspiracy, and the commentary impermissibly expands the definition beyond what the text allows, so it cannot receive deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion is disqualifying).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' means 'and' or 'or' in the safety-valve provision. The Ninth and Eleventh Circuits hold that defendants are disqualified only if they meet all three criteria, while the Fifth, Sixth, Seventh, and Eighth Circuits hold that possessing any one criterion is disqualifying. This split directly affects mandatory minimum sentencing for drug offenses.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive (all three criteria required)
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies)
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as distributive or disjunctive.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines (U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b)) when the state schedule covers substances not listed under federal law.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the career-offender guideline's definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the substance to be controlled under federal law. The Eighth Circuit holds it includes state-law offenses even for substances not federally controlled, while other circuits have required a federal-law match. This affects career-offender status and base offense levels.

positions.label: State law controls (no federal match required)
positions.summary: Holds that 'controlled substance offense' under § 4B1.2(b) broadly includes state-law drug offenses regardless of whether the particular substance is controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

positions.label: Federal law controls (federal match required)
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines only if the substance at issue is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. De La Torre

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release. Some circuits, including the Seventh and Eighth, hold that non-retroactive changes in law cannot contribute to such a finding, while the Sentencing Commission's 2023 policy statement (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) attempted to authorize it. The Seventh Circuit has held this exceeds the Commission's statutory authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether offered alone or in combination with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in law may be considered as one factor among others in the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis, consistent with the Sentencing Commission's 2023 policy statement at § 1B1.13(b)(6).

circuits.name: Sixth Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Esteras (Moore, J., dissenting)

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the appeal deadline for a Hyde Amendment attorney's fees order in a criminal case is governed by the civil deadline (60 days under FRAP 4(a)) or the criminal deadline (14 days under FRAP 4(b)).
splits.description: Circuits are split on which appeal deadline applies to orders on Hyde Amendment motions for attorney's fees. The Seventh Circuit, joining what it describes as the majority view in a 'lopsided circuit split,' holds the civil deadline applies because Hyde Amendment motions are civil matters ancillary to the criminal case. Other circuits have applied the shorter criminal deadline.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the civil appeal deadline of 60 days under FRAP 4(a) governs.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion arises in the criminal case and is governed by the shorter criminal appeal deadline of 14 days under FRAP 4(b).

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Sherburne (cited in split)

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and a good-faith conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the bankruptcy estate or the debtors. The Ninth Circuit holds such appreciation inures to the estate, while other circuits have held it belongs to the debtors. This has significant consequences for debtors who experience property appreciation during their reorganization plan.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), coupled with the broad scope of § 541(a), means that post-petition appreciation in property value belongs to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtors
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtors based on the Bankruptcy Code's structure, object, policies, and legislative history of § 348(f)(1)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release when revoking and resentencing, or whether revocation sentences must serve only the purposes of supervised release under 18 U.S.C. § 3583(e).
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit permits district courts to expressly punish defendants for supervised release violations under its precedent in United States v. Lewis, placing it at what the dissenting judges describe as 'the extreme of a circuit split.' Other circuits have held that revocation sentences may not be used to punish but only to serve the limited purposes of supervised release.

positions.label: Punishment permitted
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly impose punishment when revoking supervised release and sentencing defendants for violations.

positions.label: Punishment not permitted
positions.summary: Holds that revocation of supervised release and the resulting sentence may not be used to punish the defendant for the violation, but must instead serve only the purposes of supervised release.

circuits.name: Third Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Esteras (cited in dissent)
